name: Cova d'en Palem
starting_point: Park past Km 6 of the Ma-1043. Continue on the road about 150 m to the barrier that gives access to the path leading to the cave.
equipment: Helmet and lighting only, plus a small rope for the final ramp if wet
description: Accessed through a small 50x70 cm entrance hidden behind a strawberry tree, this cave features a main chamber of approximately 50x35 m with diverse speleothems. The deepest point (-18 m) shows evidence of ancient water levels with phreatic speleothems reminiscent of those in Cova des Pas de Vallgornera. The most decorated area contains eccentric stalactites, flowstones, gours, and a distinctive disc-shaped formation.
full_description:
Description

Sheltered by a strawberry tree, we find the small entrance of 50 x 70 cm through which we will descend without any difficulty to enter through a strenuous gallery of about nine meters in length by one and a half meters in width, in which we can never stand upright. Initially it descends gently until reaching a point where we have to get past a low section by lying flat on the ground. Right at this point, when it rains, a small puddle forms that will wet our chest as well as the elbows and knees of our coveralls, if we are wearing this garment — which I certainly recommend. Already at this point, on the right side as we advance, a narrow inaccessible crack descends 6 m; a little further on something similar occurs on both sides. Soon the gallery begins to gain height until reaching a small room of about 14 meters in length by approximately half a dozen in width, sparsely decorated except on the east side where some small stalagmites and numerous stalactites grow in a crack. Worth noting is the existence of several micro rimstone pools (gours) at the base of the largest and thickest stalagmite, which is responsible for supplying them with water. At the time of my visit on November 3, 2008, these were full of water, and all the speleothems in this corner were active. The room also contains blocks of considerable size resulting from ceiling collapses. At the other end of the room, a narrow crack separates it from the continuation of the cave except for some sections due to successive collapses.

To continue the visit, we can do so from the end of the room where, next to some formations, the opening allows us to descend a little over a meter to a dusty ramp that will deposit us on a ledge where a barrier built by the cave seems to block our way. We find ourselves in the main chamber of the cave, of considerable size — approximately 50 x 35 meters. We are in the vicinity of the most decorated area of the cave, and I will describe how to traverse it. I recommend continuing to the deepest part, always keeping the stalagmitic barrier to our right, until reaching a pair of windows, the left one being the one that allows us to continue — the right window makes it more difficult since a small column right behind the entrance obstructs our passage. Putting our feet through first, we will cross the window to access a ramp possibly covered by a film of water (depending on the season), which will not pose great difficulty to descend if we lean on a small column. Once at the bottom, we are at the deepest part of the cave at -18 m depth. It is a gallery with a very low ceiling running in an E-W direction. To reach the lowest point, we will again have to lie flat to get past a very low section and reach the sinkhole that is responsible for draining the cave's water. Two things are worth highlighting here: first, if we look carefully at the ceiling of this area, we can observe how all of it was long ago filled with water — it was therefore a lake in which curious phreatic speleothems formed, some of which are reminiscent of the famous "lightbulbs" of the Cova des Pas de Vallgornera.

To continue the visit, we will not return the way we came but rather through another sort of window found near the sinkhole, climbing up a ramp to another gallery-like passage that also runs E to W, just like the lower one. We will ascend another level where another tiny window will place us in the main chamber next to a couple of rimstone pools. Eccentric stalactites decorate the ceiling in this area along with other formations. To continue the visit, we can do so from two different spots: one is from the highest part, and the other is to the right of the window through which we arrived here. If we choose the latter option, we will again have to lie flat — or rather, lie on the floor flowstone that harbors tiny rimstone pools full of water that will wet us again. If we want to continue through the upper part of the chamber, we need only climb a ramp that will deposit us near a large rimstone pool in the most decorated area of the cave. Of note is the disc-shaped or "ensaimada"-shaped speleothem that harbors the waters of the rimstone pool. In this place we can find all types of formations, including a great variety of eccentric stalactites. Before leaving this area through a sort of doorway — at one end of which the effects of solifluction on several columns can be appreciated — we can head upward in a NW direction until reaching a stalagmitic massif next to a small room of slightly more than half a dozen meters on a lower level. On our return, we will exit through the aforementioned "doorway" in order to continue visiting the eastern area.

The time has come to prepare to "suffer" a bit. In this area the ceiling height in some places does not exceed 30 cm — floor flowstones covered with a film of water, ceilings that crumble with just the touch of a finger, dust in great abundance — in short, a bit of everything, but it is worth visiting from the bottom to the upper part, which is none other than the entrance hall or vestibule of the cavity, from which all that remains is to exit.
